content.text: Every interaction with Azure — whether through the Portal, az CLI, PowerShell, an SDK, or a direct REST call — follows the same path through Azure Resource Manager (ARM). ARM is the unified control plane that authenticates, authorizes, and routes every management request to the correct resource provider.

Understanding this flow explains why a request denied by Azure Policy shows up as a 403, why large deployments return 202 instead of 200, and why Activity Log captures every change regardless of which tool triggered it.

attrs.alt: ARM request flow: client → Azure AD → ARM → RBAC/Policy → Resource Provider → response

content.text: Step 1: Client Sends an HTTPS Request

All ARM requests target the global endpoint management.azure.com over HTTPS. The client — Portal, az CLI, PowerShell Az module, any Azure SDK, or direct REST call — sends the request with a Bearer token in the Authorization header.

# Every az CLI command becomes an ARM REST call internally
# Use --debug to see the exact HTTP request
az group create --name myRG --location eastus --debug 2>&1 | grep -E "Request URL|Method|Body"

# Direct REST equivalent
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)
curl -s -X PUT   -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"   -H "Content-Type: application/json"   -d '{"location": "eastus"}'   "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG?api-version=2021-04-01" | jq .

# Azure SDK for Python also calls management.azure.com under the hood
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = ResourceManagementClient(credential, subscription_id)

# This translates to: PUT management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG
client.resource_groups.create_or_update("myRG", {"location": "eastus"})

ARM validates the Bearer token against Azure Active Directory before processing any request. The token is a signed JWT that encodes the caller's identity, tenant, and the resource it was issued for (https://management.azure.com). ARM verifies the token signature, expiry, and audience.

# Inspect an ARM token (decode the JWT payload — never share the full token)
TOKEN=$(az account get-access-token --query accessToken -o tsv)

# Decode the payload (base64 decode the middle segment)
echo $TOKEN | cut -d'.' -f2 | base64 -d 2>/dev/null | jq '{
  oid: .oid,              # object ID of the caller (user or service principal)
  upn: .upn,              # user principal name (if user login)
  appid: .appid,          # application ID (if service principal)
  tid: .tid,              # tenant ID
  aud: .aud,              # audience — must be management.azure.com for ARM calls
  exp: .exp,              # expiry (Unix timestamp)
  scp: .scp               # scopes (for user tokens)
}'
ARM tokens expire after 1 hour by default. SDKs and CLI tools handle token refresh automatically via a credential chain. If you cache tokens manually, check the exp claim and refresh before expiry.
Token is malformed, expired, or wrong audience
Token exp claim is in the past
Token was issued for a different resource (e.g. graph.microsoft.com not management.azure.com)
Step 3: ARM Front Door — Routing and Validation

After the token is validated, ARM's front door performs request-level validation: it checks the URL format (valid subscription ID, resource group name, provider namespace), validates that the api-version exists for the requested resource type, and routes the request to the appropriate ARM region.

https://management.azure.com
  /subscriptions/{subscriptionId}           ← scope: subscription
  /resourceGroups/{resourceGroupName}       ← scope: resource group
  /providers/{providerNamespace}            ← routes to this provider
  /{resourceType}                           ← resource collection
  /{resourceName}                           ← specific resource
  ?api-version=2023-09-01                   ← required; determines schema version

Examples:
  GET  /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines
  PUT  /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa
  POST /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myVM/restart
ARM Validation Errors at This Stage
Subscription ID in the URL does not exist or caller has no access
Resource group specified does not exist
The api-version string is not recognized for this resource type
The resource type path does not exist in the provider namespace
Provider namespace is not registered in the subscription
Step 4: RBAC Authorization and Azure Policy
ARM evaluates two independent gatekeeping systems before forwarding the request to the resource provider: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and Azure Policy.

ARM checks whether the caller's identity (resolved from the JWT) has the required action permission at the target scope. If the identity lacks permission, ARM returns 403 Forbidden immediately — the request never reaches the resource provider.

# Check what actions your identity can perform on a resource
az role assignment list   --assignee $(az ad signed-in-user show --query id -o tsv)   --all   --query "[].{Role:roleDefinitionName, Scope:scope}"   --output table

# Check if a specific action is allowed (what-if for RBAC)
az role assignment list   --assignee $(az ad signed-in-user show --query id -o tsv)   --include-inherited   --query "[?contains(roleDefinitionName, 'Contributor')]"   --output table

If RBAC passes, ARM evaluates all Azure Policy assignments in effect at the subscription, resource group, and management group scopes. Policies in Deny effect block the request; Audit policies allow it through but log a compliance event.

# List policies assigned to a resource group
az policy assignment list   --resource-group myRG   --query "[].{Name:name, Policy:policyDefinitionId, Effect:parameters.effect.value}"   --output table

# Check compliance state for a resource
az policy state list   --resource /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mysa   --query "[].{Policy:policyDefinitionName, State:complianceState, Reason:stateReason}"   --output table

A 403 from ARM could mean RBAC denied the request OR Azure Policy denied it. Check the error message body — RBAC denials say 'does not have authorization to perform action'; Policy denials say 'disallowed by policy' and include the policy assignment name.
Step 5: Resource Provider Processes the Request

After passing RBAC and Policy checks, ARM forwards the request to the resource provider responsible for the namespace. The provider validates the resource-specific request body, provisions or modifies the resource, and returns the response.

Simple operations (GET, tag updates, small configuration changes) complete synchronously and return 200 OK or 201 Created with the resource JSON. Operations that take significant time (creating a VM, deploying AKS, scaling a database) are asynchronous: ARM returns 202 Accepted with headers that the client uses to poll for completion.
Implementing the LRO Polling Loop Manually
The Activity Log: Audit Trail of Every ARM Call

ARM writes a record to the Activity Log for every management operation — create, update, delete, and action operations. GET operations are not logged. The Activity Log retains records for 90 days and can be forwarded to Log Analytics, Storage, or Event Hubs for longer retention.

# View recent Activity Log entries for a resource group
az monitor activity-log list   --resource-group myRG   --start-time $(date -u -d '24 hours ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)   --query "[].{Time:eventTimestamp, Caller:caller, Operation:operationName.localizedValue, Status:status.localizedValue}"   --output table

# Find all operations by a specific user or service principal
az monitor activity-log list   --caller user@example.com   --start-time $(date -u -d '7 days ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)   --query "[].{Time:eventTimestamp, Op:operationName.value, Status:status.value, RG:resourceGroupName}"   --output table

# Find all delete operations
az monitor activity-log list   --resource-group myRG   --start-time $(date -u -d '30 days ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)   --query "[?contains(operationName.value, 'delete')].{Time:eventTimestamp, Caller:caller, Op:operationName.value}"   --output table
Forwarding Activity Logs to Log Analytics

# Create a diagnostic setting to forward Activity Log to Log Analytics
az monitor diagnostic-settings create   --name "activity-log-to-la"   --resource /subscriptions/{subscriptionId}   --workspace /subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/myWorkspace   --logs '[{"category": "Administrative", "enabled": true},
           {"category": "Security", "enabled": true},
           {"category": "Policy", "enabled": true}]'

# Query Activity Log in Log Analytics (KQL)
# AzureActivity
# | where TimeGenerated > ago(7d)
# | where OperationNameValue contains "delete"
# | project TimeGenerated, Caller, OperationNameValue, ResourceGroup, ActivityStatusValue
# | order by TimeGenerated desc

ARM enforces per-subscription, per-resource-group, and per-tenant request rate limits to protect the platform. When limits are exceeded, ARM returns 429 Too Many Requests with a Retry-After header. Well-written clients respect this header rather than retrying immediately.
ARM wraps all responses in a consistent JSON envelope. Understanding the envelope structure helps when parsing responses in scripts and when diagnosing partial failures in deployments.

# Extract error code and message from a failed az rest call
result=$(az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/nonexistent?api-version=2021-04-01"   2>&1)

error_code=$(echo "$result" | jq -r '.error.code // empty')
error_msg=$(echo "$result" | jq -r '.error.message // empty')

if [ -n "$error_code" ]; then
    echo "Error code: $error_code"
    echo "Message: $error_msg"
    exit 1
fi

import subprocess, json

def az_rest_checked(method, url):
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["az", "rest", "--method", method, "--url", url],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    data = json.loads(result.stdout or result.stderr or "{}")
    if "error" in data:
        code = data["error"].get("code", "Unknown")
        message = data["error"].get("message", "No message")
        raise RuntimeError(f"ARM error {code}: {message}")
    return data

try:
    rg = az_rest_checked("GET",
        "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/resourceGroups/myRG?api-version=2021-04-01")
    print(f"Resource group location: {rg['location']}")
except RuntimeError as e:
    print(f"Failed: {e}")

ARM list endpoints return a value array containing the resources plus an optional nextLink for pagination. The page size varies by resource type but is typically 100-1000 items. Scripts that assume a single page will silently miss resources in large subscriptions.

# List all VMs — may be paginated for large subscriptions
az rest   --method GET   --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines?api-version=2023-09-01"   | jq '{count: (.value | length), hasNextPage: (.nextLink != null)}'

# Follow pagination manually
PAGE_URL="https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines?api-version=2023-09-01"
while [ -n "$PAGE_URL" ]; do
    PAGE=$(az rest --method GET --url "$PAGE_URL")
    echo "$PAGE" | jq '.value[].name'
    PAGE_URL=$(echo "$PAGE" | jq -r '.nextLink // empty')
done

import subprocess, json

def list_all(url):
    """Collect all items from a paginated ARM collection endpoint."""
    items = []
    while url:
        result = subprocess.run(
            ["az", "rest", "--method", "GET", "--url", url],
            capture_output=True, text=True, check=True
        )
        page = json.loads(result.stdout)
        items.extend(page.get("value", []))
        url = page.get("nextLink")
    return items

# Collect all storage accounts across a subscription
all_storage = list_all(
    "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{id}/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts?api-version=2023-01-01"
)
print(f"Total storage accounts: {len(all_storage)}")
ARM Deployment Operations vs Direct Resource Operations

There are two ways to create or update resources through ARM: direct resource operations (PUT to the resource URL) and deployment operations (PUT to a deployments URL with a template body). The Portal and Bicep/ARM templates use the deployment path; az resource create and SDK calls can use either.

# List all operations from a specific deployment
az deployment operation group list   --resource-group myRG   --name myDeployment   --query "[].{Resource:properties.targetResource.resourceType, Name:properties.targetResource.resourceName, State:properties.provisioningState}"   --output table

# Get details of failed operations
az deployment operation group list   --resource-group myRG   --name myDeployment   --query "[?properties.provisioningState=='Failed'].properties.statusMessage"   --output json | jq '.[] | {code: .error.code, message: .error.message}'
Template What-If to Preview ARM Changes

# Preview what an ARM deployment would change — no resources are modified
az deployment group what-if   --resource-group myRG   --template-file main.bicep   --parameters @params.json

# What-if output categories:
#   Create   — resource does not exist and will be created
#   Modify   — resource exists and properties will change
#   Delete   — resource exists and will be removed (Complete mode only)
#   NoChange — resource exists and matches the template exactly
#   Ignore   — resource is outside the template scope
Tracing an ARM Request End to End

When debugging a failing deployment or unexpected behavior, combining the Activity Log, deployment operation history, and az rest lets you trace exactly what happened at each layer of the ARM request flow.
Check the Activity Log for the operation and its status (caller, timestamp, result).
If it's a deployment, check the deployment operation list for which resource failed.
Use az rest with the exact resource URL to GET the current state and provisioningState.
Check Azure Policy compliance state if the failure is authorization-related.
Check RBAC assignments if the failure is permission-related.

#!/bin/bash
# Complete ARM debug script for a failed resource
SUBSCRIPTION_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)
RG="myRG"
DEPLOYMENT="myDeployment"

echo "=== 1. Activity Log (last 1h) ==="
az monitor activity-log list   --resource-group "$RG"   --start-time "$(date -u -d '1 hour ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"   --query "[?status.value!='Started'].{Time:eventTimestamp, Op:operationName.value, Status:status.value, Caller:caller}"   --output table

echo ""
echo "=== 2. Deployment Operation Status ==="
az deployment operation group list   --resource-group "$RG"   --name "$DEPLOYMENT"   --query "[].{Resource:properties.targetResource.resourceType, State:properties.provisioningState, Error:properties.statusMessage.error.message}"   --output table 2>/dev/null || echo "No deployment named $DEPLOYMENT found"

echo ""
echo "=== 3. Resource Group Resources ==="
az resource list   --resource-group "$RG"   --query "[].{Name:name, Type:type, State:properties.provisioningState}"   --output table

echo ""
echo "=== 4. Policy Compliance ==="
az policy state list   --resource-group "$RG"   --query "[?complianceState!='Compliant'].{Policy:policyDefinitionName, Resource:resourceId, State:complianceState}"   --output table 2>/dev/null || echo "No policy compliance data"
ARM Request Flow Differences by Tool
Browser OAuth2 flow — user signs in
Polling UI shown in Notifications panel
az login OAuth2 or SP env vars
Blocks until LRO completes (--no-wait to skip)
SP or user depending on token
OIDC federated token or SP secret
Handled by SDK internals in action steps
Handled by az CLI in steps

Every Azure management operation follows the same five-stage ARM request flow: HTTPS to management.azure.com, Azure AD token validation, ARM routing and URL validation, RBAC and Azure Policy authorization, then the resource provider handles provisioning. Long-running operations use the 202 + polling pattern with Azure-AsyncOperation or Location headers. The Activity Log records every write operation, providing a subscription-wide audit trail. Understanding this flow helps diagnose authentication failures, permission denials, policy blocks, async timeouts, and rate limiting — the most common categories of ARM errors in production.
